mappings.rationaleAI: The Sun's composition, photosphere, chromosphere, corona, sunspots, solar cycle, solar wind, and expected evolution comprehensively address all aspects of the Sun (B).

The solar wind as a stream of charged particles from the corona, its interaction with Earth's magnetosphere, and the heliosphere address the corona and solar wind (B.1.c), solar-terrestrial relationships (B.5), and the interplanetary medium (A.3.d).

A survey of all eight planets, their characteristics, formation, and the reclassification of Pluto comprehensively address the solar system survey (A) and both terrestrial (C.1) and giant planets (C.3).
Saturn's gas giant composition, ring system, moons (Titan, Enceladus), and Cassini-Huygens discoveries directly address Saturn (C.3.b).
Jupiter's composition, Great Red Spot, Galilean satellites, magnetosphere, and role in solar system dynamics directly address Jupiter (C.3.a).
Mercury's extreme conditions, cratered surface, large iron core, and MESSENGER mission directly address Mercury (C.1.a).
Venus's extreme conditions, runaway greenhouse effect, retrograde rotation, and Venera missions directly address Venus (C.1.b).
Asteroids' classification, composition, distribution in the main belt, and role as remnants of solar system formation directly address minor planets/asteroids (A.3.a).
Comets' composition, Oort Cloud and Kuiper Belt origins, orbital mechanics, and role in delivering water to early Earth directly address comets (A.3.b).
The Kuiper Belt beyond Neptune, Pluto's reclassification, trans-Neptunian objects, and short-period comet origins address comets' source regions (A.3.b) and Pluto (C.3.e).
Solar and lunar eclipse mechanics, the Babylonian saros cycle, and Eddington's 1919 expedition directly address eclipses of the Sun and Moon (E.7.b).
The Moon's giant impact origin, geological composition, tidal effects, Apollo missions, and role in stabilising Earth's axial tilt comprehensively address the Moon (E).

Earth's formation from the nebular hypothesis, accretion of planetesimals, the Moon-forming impact, and differentiation of core/mantle/crust address theories of solar system origin (A.6) and the Earth as a planet (D).

The Earth's core composition (iron and nickel), magnetic field generation through dynamo theory, and core-mantle interactions address the Earth's physical properties (D.4) and interior structure (D.5).
Cosmic rays as high-energy particles in the interplanetary medium address the interplanetary medium (A.3.d).
Habitability of planets including Mars, Europa, and Enceladus as candidates for life addresses theories of solar system evolution (A.6) and Mars specifically (C.1.d).
